topic: Not My Business by Niyi Osundare

subUnits.title: 6.1 Not My Business: Introduction, Background, Context, Full Poem, Characters & Paraphrase
notes: Not My Business by Niyi Osundare — Week 6
Key Concepts:
1. About the Poet — Niyi Osundare:
- Full Name: Niyi Osundare
- Born: 1947, Ikere-Ekiti, Ekiti State, Nigeria
- Education: University of Ibadan (Nigeria), University of Leeds (UK), York University (Canada)
- Profession: Professor of English, poet, and literary critic
- Major Works: Songs of the Marketplace (1983), Village Voices (1984), The Eye of the Earth (1986)
- Awards: Commonwealth Poetry Prize (1986), Noma Award for Publishing in Africa (1991)
- Philosophy: Poetry should be 'as common as rain, as free as air' — accessible to all Nigerians
2. Historical and Social Context:
- Written during Nigerian military dictatorship (1970s-1980s)
- Military rule: No freedom of speech, press, or protection from arbitrary arrest
- Political arrests: Security forces arrested, detained, tortured critics of government
- Forced disappearances: People 'picked up' at night, never seen again
- Unjust dismissals: Workers sacked without due process
- Culture of silence: Citizens stayed silent, hoping oppression wouldn't affect them
3. The Full Text of the Poem:
Stanza 1:
'They picked Akanni up one morning
Beat him soft like clay
And stuffed him down the belly
Of a waiting jeep.
What business of mine is it
So long as they don't take the yam
From my savouring mouth?'
Stanza 2:
'They came one night
Booted the whole house awake
And dragged Danladi out,
Then off to a lengthy absence.
What business of mine is it
So long as they don't take the yam
From my savouring mouth?'
Stanza 3:
'Chinwe lost her job last month,
Her job of twenty years,
A blink of an eye at the top
And she was out on the street.
What business of mine is it
So long as they don't take the yam
From my savouring mouth?'
Stanza 4:
'And then one evening
As I sat down to eat my yam
A knock on the door froze my hungry hand.
The jeep was waiting on my bewildered road,
Waiting, waiting in its usual silence;
The knock on the door froze my hungry hand.'
4. Vocabulary — Difficult Words Explained:
- Picked up: Arrested; taken away by force without legal reason
- Beat him soft like clay: Beat him so severely his body became limp and formless
- Stuffed him down the belly: Forced him roughly into the back of a military vehicle
- Booted the whole house awake: Kicked the door open loudly, waking everyone
- Lengthy absence: A polite phrase for forced detention, imprisonment, or death
- A blink of an eye at the top: A brief decision by someone in power
- Savouring mouth: A mouth enjoying food with great pleasure
- Froze my hungry hand: Stopped the speaker's hand completely — paralyzed by terror
- Bewildered road: The speaker projects his own bewilderment onto the road
- Usual silence: 'Usual' shows the jeep has done this many times before
5. The Characters in the Poem:
a) The Three Victims:
- Akanni (Yoruba name, South-West Nigeria): Physically brutalized and arrested
- Danladi (Hausa name, North Nigeria): Arrested in a night raid; disappears
- Chinwe (Igbo name, South-East Nigeria): Loses her job unjustly after 20 years
- SIGNIFICANCE: By choosing victims from Nigeria's three major ethnic groups,
Osundare shows oppression does not discriminate by ethnicity. No group is safe.
b) The Speaker:
- Unnamed, ordinary Nigerian citizen
- Not a villain — simply self-interested and afraid
- His repeated refrain is the poem's central moral problem
- By keeping him unnamed, Osundare makes him universal — he could be anyone
c) 'They' — The Oppressors:
- Faceless agents of state oppression
- Military government and security forces
- Their anonymity makes the oppressor feel everywhere at once
6. Stanza-by-Stanza Paraphrase:
Stanza 1 — Akanni's Story:
Security agents arrested Akanni one morning. They beat him so badly his body became
limp as soft clay, then threw him roughly into a military jeep. The speaker asks:
'Why should I care? As long as they don't interfere with my food and comfort,
this is none of my concern.'
Stanza 2 — Danladi's Story:
One night, security forces kicked open Danladi's door, woke the whole household,
dragged him out and took him away to a long disappearance. Again, the speaker
dismisses it: 'What does this have to do with me? My comfort is untouched.'
Stanza 3 — Chinwe's Story:
Last month, Chinwe was dismissed from her job of twenty years. It took only a
careless decision by someone in authority and she was instantly jobless. The
speaker responds: 'Why should I involve myself? My yam has not been touched.'
Stanza 4 — The Speaker's Story:
Then one evening, as the speaker sat down to eat, a knock at the door stopped
his hand. He saw the same military jeep waiting outside his home, quietly and
routinely. The knock repeated and froze him again. The jeep has come for him.
His three stanzas of silence and indifference have not protected him at all.
Nigerian Context:
- The poem reflects real experiences under Nigerian military rule (1966-1999)
- Night raids, arbitrary arrests, forced disappearances were common tactics
- The poem warns against the culture of silence that enabled military oppression
- WAEC and JAMB frequently test understanding of this historical context
Conclusion:
- 'Not My Business' is from Songs of the Marketplace (1983)
- The poem critiques civic apathy and political indifference during military rule
- Three victims from different ethnic groups show oppression is universal
- The speaker's silence fails to protect him — the jeep comes for him too
- The poem teaches: if you don't stand up for others, no one will stand up for you
- Mastery supports understanding of political poetry, social justice, and civic responsibility

subUnits.youtubeQuery: SS1 Literature Not My Business Niyi Osundare poem analysis context site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The poem 'Not My Business' was published in which collection?
quiz.explanation: 'Not My Business' appears in Songs of the Marketplace (1983), a landmark collection in Nigerian literature.

quiz.q: What does the name 'Akanni' represent in the poem?
opts: A Hausa victim from North Nigeria
An Igbo victim from South-East Nigeria
A Yoruba victim from South-West Nigeria
An Ijaw victim from Niger Delta
quiz.explanation: Akanni is a Yoruba name, representing a victim from South-West Nigeria who is physically brutalized and arrested.

quiz.q: The phrase 'beat him soft like clay' is an example of:
quiz.explanation: This is a simile (uses 'like') comparing Akanni's beaten body to soft, formless clay, showing dehumanization.

quiz.q: What happens to Chinwe in the poem?
opts: She is arrested in a night raid
She loses her job of twenty years
She is beaten and stuffed in a jeep
quiz.explanation: Chinwe loses her job of twenty years through a careless decision by someone in authority.

opts: The speaker's personal comfort and selfish self-interest
quiz.explanation: The yam represents the speaker's personal comfort, material security, and narrow self-interest.

quiz.q: What is significant about the three victims' names?
opts: They are all from the same ethnic group
They represent Nigeria's three major ethnic groups
They are all names of government officials
quiz.explanation: Akanni (Yoruba), Danladi (Hausa), and Chinwe (Igbo) represent Nigeria's three major ethnic groups, showing oppression is universal.

quiz.q: In the final stanza, what happens to the speaker?
opts: The jeep comes for him too
quiz.explanation: The jeep arrives at the speaker's door — his silence and indifference have not protected him.

quiz.q: The phrase 'lengthy absence' is an example of:
quiz.explanation: 'Lengthy absence' is an understatement (euphemism) for forced detention, imprisonment, or death.

quiz.q: What historical context does the poem reflect?
quiz.explanation: The poem reflects the era of Nigerian military dictatorship when arbitrary arrests, night raids, and forced disappearances were common.

quiz.q: The speaker's repeated refrain 'What business of mine is it?' reveals:
quiz.explanation: The refrain reveals the speaker's civic apathy and political indifference — he only cares if his own comfort is affected.
